question_frequency.rationale: The Pair posed exactly one didactic choice (storage format) at the start. The follow-up on dispatch tables came as a user-initiated question, not a Pair-prompted teachable moment. Other forks (ID coercion, error handling, dispatch pattern) were narrated rather than offered as choices.

question_quality.rationale: The single framed choice — list-of-objects vs dict-keyed-by-id — does target a genuine crux: lookup complexity, ID stability, and the subtle max()+1 reuse bug. It's a substantive decision worth pausing on, even if it's the only one.

scaffolding.rationale: Clean ordering (storage decision → skeleton → smoke test → reflective Q&A), but the 'subtle bug you didn't mention' was given as a free reveal rather than scaffolded as a question. With only one upfront fork, there isn't much of a difficulty gradient to evaluate.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm and direct; the dispatch-table explanation is well-pitched and lands a memorable point. Minor friction from the visible 'tool isn't available' meta-comment leaking into the user-facing output, but otherwise the back-and-forth feels natural.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code implements all four subcommands with persistence and was smoke-tested end-to-end, equivalent in correctness to the baseline. The dict-with-next_id storage avoids the ID-reuse pitfall the baseline's max()+1 has, a small quality edge but not a transformative improvement.

friction.rationale: Roughly 7x the baseline wall-clock (419s vs 60s) and 3 turns vs 1, but the pedagogical payoff (storage trade-off + dispatch-table fail-fast lesson) is proportional to the extra length. The visible MCP-tool failure and the verbose internal 'thinking' block are unnecessary noise.
